questions.question: Job scheduler at scale like Clockwork
questions.answer:
A job scheduler at scale needs distributed coordination, fault tolerance, and efficient task execution. Use a database-backed scheduler with leader election for single-point-of-failure prevention. Quartz with Spring can be clustered using database clustering. For massive scale (100K+ jobs), use Apache Airflow or self-built solutions with Kafka topics as job queues.

Key considerations: (1) Distributed locking to prevent duplicate execution (SELECT FOR UPDATE or distributed locks via Redis), (2) Retry logic with exponential backoff, (3) Partitioning jobs across multiple scheduler instances, (4) Dead letter queues for failed jobs, (5) Observability with job execution metrics.

Implementation pattern: Store job metadata in a central DB. Each scheduler node polls for pending jobs, acquires a lock, executes, and records completion. Failed jobs get rescheduled with backoff. For Clockwork-like scale, partition jobs by time windows (minute-level buckets) and distribute across nodes.
questions.diagrams:
Distributed Scheduler Architecture:
[Scheduler Node 1] --DB Lock--> [Shared Job Queue DB]
[Scheduler Node 2] --DB Lock--> [Shared Job Queue DB]
[Scheduler Node 3] --DB Lock--> [Shared Job Queue DB]
       ^
       |
    Leader Election (Redis/DB)
       |
       v
[Dead Letter Queue] <-- Failed Jobs (after retries)

questions.question: Metrics platform for SLOs
questions.answer:
An SLO (Service Level Objective) metrics platform tracks system reliability against defined goals. It aggregates metrics from all services, calculates error budgets, and alerts when SLOs are breached. Build using Prometheus for metrics collection, Grafana for visualization, and custom logic to compute SLO compliance.

Core components: (1) Metrics sources (applications push/Prometheus scrapes), (2) Time-series database (Prometheus/InfluxDB), (3) Aggregation layer (calculate success rates, latency percentiles), (4) Alert engine (breach detection), (5) Dashboard (SLO status, error budget remaining).

SLO calculation: Track total requests, failed requests, slow requests (>threshold). Calculate success_rate = (total - failed - slow) / total. If success_rate < SLO_target, alert. Error budget = (1 - SLO) * total_requests_in_period. Once budget exhausted, freeze deployments.
questions.diagrams:
SLO Platform Flow:
Applications --> [Metrics Export] --> Prometheus Server
                                           |
                                           v
                                    [Time-Series DB]
                                           |
                      _____________________|_____________________
                      |                     |                    |
                      v                     v                    v
               SLO Calculator         Grafana Dashboard    Alert Engine
                      |
         [Error Budget Tracker]
                      |
         Breach --> [Slack Alert]

questions.question: HashMap vs ConcurrentHashMap, equals/hashCode contract, immutability
questions.answer:
HashMap is not thread-safe but faster for single-threaded use. ConcurrentHashMap is thread-safe using segment-level locking (Java 7) or node-level locking (Java 8+), sacrificing some speed for concurrency.

The equals/hashCode contract: If two objects are equal (obj1.equals(obj2) == true), they MUST have the same hashCode. Violating this breaks HashMap/HashSet lookups. Objects used as map keys should be immutable to prevent hash value changes after insertion, which can lose the object in the map.

Immutability prevents concurrent modification issues. Immutable keys (String, Integer) ensure their hashCode never changes, making them safe for HashMap keys. Mutable keys like Date can change their hashCode if modified, orphaning them in the map.
questions.diagrams:
HashMap vs ConcurrentHashMap (Java 8+):
HAASHMAP: [Single Lock on entire map]
          No thread safety
          Faster for single-threaded
          
ConcurrentHashMap: [Node 1][Node 2][Node 3][Node 4]
                      ^       ^        ^       ^
                   Lock L1  Lock L2  Lock L3  Lock L4
                   
Multiple threads can lock different nodes simultaneously

questions.question: Threads vs executors, JMM basics, GC tuning (G1/ZGC talk-throughs)
questions.answer:
Threads are OS-level constructs, expensive to create. Executors (ThreadPool) reuse threads via queues, reducing overhead. Java Memory Model (JMM) defines visibility and ordering: volatile ensures visibility, synchronized provides both visibility and atomicity, happens-before relationships guarantee memory operations order.

GC tuning trade-offs: G1GC targets low latency with predictable pause times by cleaning regions incrementally. ZGC achieves ultra-low pauses (<10ms) using concurrent mark/compact. G1 is default for most apps. ZGC requires more CPU overhead but ideal for latency-sensitive systems (trading throughput for latency).

JMM guarantee: All threads see the latest value of a volatile variable. All operations in a synchronized block appear atomic. Without these, thread A's writes might not be visible to thread B on different CPU cores due to CPU cache optimization.
questions.diagrams:
JMM Happens-Before Relationships:
[Thread A writes x]
        |
   Lock/Volatile Release
        |
   Memory Barrier
        |
   Lock/Volatile Acquire
        |
[Thread B reads x] <- Guaranteed to see latest value

G1 vs ZGC Pause Time vs Throughput:
        Throughput
          /
         / G1GC
        /
       /_____ ZGC (low pause, high CPU)
      /
Latency ----->

questions.question: Java 8/11/17 features in practice (streams, records, var, switch, text blocks)
questions.answer:
Streams (Java 8): Enable functional-style data processing with lazy evaluation and built-in parallelization. Records (Java 14/17): Simplify immutable data classes, auto-generating equals/hashCode/toString. var (Java 10): Local variable type inference reduces boilerplate. Switch expressions (Java 12+): Cleaner than switch statements, returns values. Text blocks (Java 13+): Multiline strings without escape sequences.

Streams: Use for transformations (filter, map) but avoid for imperative logic. Parallel streams use ForkJoinPool but incur overhead for small datasets. Records eliminate boilerplate but cannot extend classes (only interfaces). var improves readability in complex generics but can obscure types. Switch expressions guarantee exhaustiveness checks at compile-time.
questions.diagrams:
Java Version Feature Timeline:
Java 8  --> Streams, Lambdas, Optional
Java 10 --> var (local type inference)
Java 12 --> Switch Expressions
Java 13 --> Text Blocks
Java 14 --> Records (preview)
Java 17 --> Records (final), Sealed Classes

Stream Processing Pipeline:
input --> [filter] --> [map] --> [reduce] --> output
           (lazy)     (lazy)    (terminal)

questions.question: Resilience in prod: retries, timeouts, circuit breakers (what + where)
questions.answer:
Resilience prevents cascading failures. Retries: Retry transient failures (network blips) with exponential backoff. Avoid retrying non-idempotent operations without deduplication. Timeouts: Prevent indefinite hangs; set on all external calls (HTTP, DB queries). Fail fast rather than wait forever.

Circuit Breaker: Detects repeated failures and stops sending requests to failing service, preventing resource exhaustion. States: CLOSED (normal), OPEN (failing, reject requests), HALF_OPEN (test if recovered). Resilience4j provides these patterns with annotations.

Where to apply: (1) HTTP calls between microservices, (2) Database queries with potential slowness, (3) Message queue sends, (4) Cache operations. Don't apply to: local in-process calls, already-synchronous request processing.
questions.diagrams:
Circuit Breaker State Transitions:
      [CLOSED] ---> [OPEN]
        ^            |
        |            | (wait duration)
        |            v
        +--- [HALF_OPEN] -> success -> [CLOSED]
                          -> failure -> [OPEN]
                          
Retry with Exponential Backoff:
Attempt 1 (immediate)
Attempt 2 (wait 1s)
Attempt 3 (wait 2s)
Attempt 4 (wait 4s)
Fail after max retries

questions.question: Spring Boot auto-config, configuration profiles, Actuator/health
questions.answer:
Spring Boot AutoConfiguration uses @ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnProperty to conditionally create beans based on classpath and config. SpringFactoriesLoader loads configurations from META-INF/spring.factories. Profiles (dev, prod, test) let you load different beans and configs. application-{profile}.yml overrides application.yml.

Actuator exposes operational endpoints: /health (liveness/readiness), /metrics, /env. Health indicators report service status. Custom indicators implement HealthIndicator. Readiness probe checks if service can handle requests. Liveness probe checks if service is still running.

Order matters: Framework loads beans in order, later @Configuration classes can override earlier ones using @ConditionalOnMissingBean.
questions.diagrams:
Spring Boot Startup Flow:
[Application Start]
         |
    [Classpath Scan]
         |
    [Load spring.factories]
         |
    [Execute AutoConfiguration Classes]
         |
    [Check @Conditional annotations]
         |
    [Register Beans]
         |
    [Call @PostConstruct]
         |
    [Application Ready]

questions.question: REST design: validation, versioning, idempotency; SAGA/outbox patterns
questions.answer:
REST validation: Use @Valid on @RequestBody, Bean Validation annotations (@NotNull, @Min, @Pattern). Return 400 Bad Request with details. Versioning: URL path (/api/v1/users) or header (Accept: application/vnd.company.v1+json) based on API stability. Idempotency: Duplicate requests should return same result. Use idempotency keys (UUID) stored in DB to detect and skip duplicates.

SAGA pattern: Distributed transaction across services. Choreography (events) vs Orchestration (coordinator). Outbox pattern: Service writes both data change and event to same DB transaction, ensuring exactly-once delivery. Separate process polls outbox and publishes to event bus.
questions.diagrams:
API Versioning Strategies:
Path: /api/v1/users  vs  /api/v2/users
Header: Accept: application/vnd.api.v1+json

SAGA Choreography vs Orchestration:
Choreography (Events):
[Order Service] --OrderCreated--> [Payment Service] --PaymentDone--> [Inventory Service]

Orchestration (Coordinator):
[Order Saga Orchestrator]
   |         |         |
   v         v         v
[Order] [Payment] [Inventory]

Outbox Pattern:
[Create Order] + [Write to Outbox] --> Same Transaction
                     |
              [Outbox Poller] --> [Event Bus] --> Other Services

questions.question: Kafka (ordering, retries, DLQs) and RabbitMQ (work queues) in payments flows
questions.answer:
Kafka: Partition-level ordering guarantees (same key goes to same partition). Use single partition for strict ordering per entity (payment ID as key). Retries via consumer group rebalance and offset reset. DLQs: Send failed messages after max retries to separate topic for manual review.

RabbitMQ: Traditional work queue with acknowledgments. Messages stay in queue until consumer ACKs (no automatic deletion). Failed messages can be republished or sent to DLQ exchange. Less ordering guarantee than Kafka; suitable for async tasks.

Payment flow best practice: Use Kafka for event sourcing (all payment events immutable). Use RabbitMQ for task distribution (payment retry jobs). Idempotency: Message ID tracking prevents duplicate processing.
questions.diagrams:
Kafka Topic with Partitions (Ordering):
Topic: payments
[Partition 0] -- Orders from Customer A (Key=A)
[Partition 1] -- Orders from Customer B (Key=B)
[Partition 2] -- Orders from Customer C (Key=C)

Same customer's orders always go to same partition --> Ordering guaranteed

Kafka DLQ Flow:
[Kafka Topic] --> [Consumer Retries 3x]
                        |
                   Failed
                        |
                   [DLQ Topic] --> Manual Review

questions.question: Observability: metrics/tracing; dealing with 300B+ metrics/day style scale
questions.answer:
Observability (metrics, logs, traces) answers "why" production behaves as observed. Metrics: Counter (requests_total), Gauge (memory_usage), Histogram (latency distribution). Distributed tracing: Tracks request across services with unique trace IDs, showing latency per service.

At 300B metrics/day scale: (1) Cardinality explosion prevention - avoid unbounded label values (request body content as metric label), (2) Sampling - trace only 1% of requests, (3) Metric rollup - keep 1-minute resolution only, not second-level, (4) Separate hot-path and cold-path tracing.

Tools: Prometheus (metrics), Jaeger/Zipkin (distributed tracing), ELK (logs). Baggage: Pass metadata (user_id, region) across trace without instrumenting all services.
questions.diagrams:
Observability Stack:
[Applications] 
     |
     |--[Metrics] --> Prometheus --> Grafana
     |--[Logs] --> ELK Stack
     |--[Traces] --> Jaeger/Zipkin
     |
[Alert Manager] <-- Prometheus Rules

Distributed Trace Flow:
User Request (Trace-ID: abc123)
   |
   v
[API Gateway] (Span 1: 10ms)
   |
   +-- [Order Service] (Span 2: 50ms)
   |     |
   |     +-- [Payment Service] (Span 3: 30ms)
   |     +-- [Inventory Service] (Span 4: 20ms)
   |
Total: ~110ms

questions.question: Index design & trade-offs; deduping rows; window functions vs GROUP BY
questions.answer:
Index design: B-tree indexes for range queries (WHERE age > 30). Hash indexes for exact match (WHERE id = 5). Composite indexes (col1, col2) support queries on both columns in order, but col2 query without col1 doesn't use index. Trade-off: Faster reads, slower writes (must update index). Avoid over-indexing.

Deduping: Use GROUP BY with aggregate (COUNT(*)), or window functions (ROW_NUMBER() PARTITION BY col ORDER BY col) to tag duplicates, then filter WHERE rn = 1. Window functions superior when you need all columns from duplicate groups.

Window functions vs GROUP BY: GROUP BY returns 1 row per group. Window functions return 1 row per original row with aggregate values attached. Use window functions for ranking, running totals.
questions.code_example:
-- Index examples
CREATE INDEX idx_user_age ON users(age); -- B-tree
CREATE INDEX idx_user_email_created ON users(email, created_at); -- Composite

-- Dedup with window function
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT *,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY email ORDER BY created_at DESC) as rn
  FROM users
) t WHERE rn = 1; -- Keep latest per email

-- Window function vs GROUP BY
-- Window: Returns all columns, keeps original rows
SELECT 
  order_id, customer_id, amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id) as total_spent
FROM orders;

-- GROUP BY: One row per group
SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) as total_spent
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id;
questions.diagrams:
B-tree Index Structure:
        [50]
       /    \
     [25]  [75]
    /  \   /  \
   [10][40][60][90]

Composite Index (col1, col2):
Index supports:
  - WHERE col1 = X (uses index)
  - WHERE col1 = X AND col2 = Y (uses index)
  - WHERE col2 = Y (doesn't use index)

Window Function Output:
Original:  customer_id | amount | total
           A           | 100    | 300
           A           | 200    | 300
           B           | 150    | 150

All 3 rows returned with aggregates

questions.question: Transaction isolation, pagination strategies; read-vs-write models for feeds
questions.answer:
Transaction isolation levels: READ_UNCOMMITTED (dirty reads), READ_COMMITTED (no dirty, but non-repeatable), REPEATABLE_READ (no non-repeatable, but phantom), SERIALIZABLE (no anomalies, slowest). Use REPEATABLE_READ for most apps; SERIALIZABLE for critical transactions.

Pagination: OFFSET/LIMIT works but slow for large offsets (must scan all rows). Use keyset pagination: WHERE id > last_id LIMIT 20 (efficient, O(1)). For deep pagination (trending feeds), use search indexes (Elasticsearch).

Read vs Write models: CQRS - separate DB for reads (denormalized, optimized) vs writes (normalized, transactional). Example: Write to normalized user table, async replicate to denormalized feed table. Adds eventual consistency but reads are fast.
questions.diagrams:
Pagination Strategies:
OFFSET: SELECT * FROM orders OFFSET 50000 LIMIT 20 -- Scans 50000 rows!

Keyset: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id > 999 LIMIT 20 -- Index seek, fast!

CQRS Architecture:
[Write Request]
      |
      v
[Normalized DB (transactional)]
      |
      | Event
      v
[Event Bus]
      |
      v
[Denormalized Read DB] <-- [Read Queries]

questions.question: Why can a Stream be consumed only once? What would happen if we try to reuse the same stream?
questions.answer:
Streams are lazy, stateful pipelines that can only be traversed once. After a terminal operation (collect, forEach, reduce), the stream is exhausted and cannot be reused. Attempting to reuse throws IllegalStateException: "stream has already been operated upon or closed".

Reason: Streams represent a sequence of operations on data, not the data itself. Once iterated, the iterator is consumed. Reusing would require storing all intermediate results or replaying the pipeline, which defeats the purpose of lazy evaluation and memory efficiency.

Solution: If you need multiple passes, either store results in a collection or create a new stream from source each time. Don't try to share stream references across methods.
questions.diagrams:
Stream Consumption Lifecycle:
[Stream Created] --> [Operations Added (lazy, no execution)]
                          |
                    [Terminal Operation]
                          |
                    [Pipeline Executes]
                          |
                  [Stream EXHAUSTED]
                          |
                    [Cannot Reuse]

questions.question: Why do we need Stream if we can write code without stream API?
questions.answer:
Streams provide functional programming benefits: (1) Conciseness - filter/map/reduce in 1 expression vs 5+ lines of loop code, (2) Readability - declarative what vs imperative how, (3) Parallelization - parallel() for multi-threaded processing with minimal code, (4) Lazy evaluation - operations execute only when needed, (5) Composability - chain operations naturally.

Without streams, you write imperative loops with temporary variables, manual iteration state, and nested conditions. Streams encourage immutability and avoid side effects, reducing bugs.

However, streams aren't always better: complex imperative logic is clearer as loops. Simple single-pass transformations benefit from streams.
questions.diagrams:
Stream vs Loop Complexity:
Loop: Manual state, mutation, iteration logic
      Sum = 0; Iterate; Check; Transform; Accumulate
      
Stream: Declarative intent
       filter -> map -> reduce
       
Parallel Streams: Automatic work distribution
       [ForkJoinPool divides data]
            |
    [Thread 1] [Thread 2] [Thread 3]
            |
       [Combine results]

questions.question: Can you explain Stream Pipeline Structure?
questions.answer:
Stream pipeline consists of three parts: (1) Source (collection, array, generator), (2) Intermediate operations (filter, map, flatMap, distinct, sorted - all lazy, return Stream), (3) Terminal operation (forEach, collect, reduce, findFirst - triggers execution, returns result or void).

Pipeline execution is lazy: intermediate operations build up a chain without executing. Only when a terminal operation is called does the entire pipeline execute. Execution is sequential by default; parallelStream() distributes work across threads.

Intermediate operations are stateless or stateful. Stateless (filter, map) process each element independently. Stateful (sorted, distinct) must examine all elements before proceeding.
questions.diagrams:
Stream Pipeline Execution Flow:
Source: [A, B, C, D, E]
         |
     filter(>2 chars)  [Lazy - no execution yet]
         |
     map(toUpper)      [Lazy]
         |
     collect()         [TERMINAL - Executes entire pipeline]
         |
    [A', C', D', E'] (filtered & mapped)

Execution Order (intermediate operations process one element at a time):
A -> filter -> map -> output
B -> filter -> map -> output
...

Not: filter all -> map all -> output

questions.question: Why are intermediate operations called lazy?
questions.answer:
Intermediate operations (filter, map, flatMap) don't execute immediately when called. They only set up a chain of transformations. Execution is deferred until a terminal operation (collect, forEach, reduce) is called. This is lazy evaluation.

Benefit: (1) Efficiency - if you filter but never consume result, no work is done. (2) Composability - build complex pipelines step-by-step. (3) Short-circuit - if findFirst() matches on 2nd element, later operations don't run on remaining elements.

Downside: Debugging is harder (set breakpoints in terminal operation). Side effects in intermediate operations may not execute if terminal is absent.
questions.diagrams:
Lazy Evaluation Timeline:
[Build Pipeline] --> No execution
filter --> Added to chain
map --> Added to chain
reduce --> TERMINAL OPERATION CALLED --> [EXECUTE PIPELINE]
           |
           v
[Process element 1]
Process element 2]
[Process element 3]
[Return result]

questions.question: If a stream pipeline contains multiple filter() operations, does it iterate the collection multiple times? Explain.
questions.answer:
No, the collection is iterated only once. Each element flows through the entire pipeline (filter1 -> filter2 -> filter3) before the next element starts. This is called "one-pass" evaluation. If you had three separate loops, collection would be iterated 3 times.

Optimization: Filters are evaluated left-to-right in order. If filter1 rejects element, it's never passed to filter2 (short-circuit). For performance, put most selective filters first to reduce elements passing through.
questions.diagrams:
Single-Pass Multi-Filter Execution:
Collection: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
             |
        [filter1] --> false (1 rejected)
        [filter1] --> true
             |
        [filter2] --> true (2 passes both)
        [filter1] --> true
             |
        [filter2] --> true (3 passes both)
        [filter1] --> true
             |
        [filter2] --> false (4 rejected)
        [filter1] --> false (5 rejected)

Result: [2, 3]

Time: O(n) single iteration, not O(3n)

questions.question: You are processing a list of 10 million records using Streams. After applying multiple filters and a map operation, the performance becomes slow. What factors would you check to identify the problem?
questions.answer:
Check: (1) Filter ordering - put most selective first, (2) Lazy evaluation being used - ensure terminal operation exists, (3) Expensive operations in intermediate steps (map transformation cost), (4) Memory allocation - if collecting all results in List, 10M objects in memory, (5) Parallel vs sequential - parallelStream() overhead may exceed benefit for 10M elements if filters are cheap, (6) Stateful operations (sorted, distinct) before collecting - these must examine all elements.

Optimizations: (1) Stream only required data subset before collecting, (2) Use Iterator pattern for streaming to disk (no in-memory collection), (3) Consider batch processing (1M at a time), (4) Profile with JFR (Java Flight Recorder) to find hot spots.
questions.diagrams:
Filter Ordering Impact:
Slow Order: [10M] -> filter1 (90%) -> [9M] -> filter2 (50%) -> [4.5M]
            Processes 10M + 9M + 4.5M = 23.5M operations

Fast Order: [10M] -> filter2 (50%) -> [5M] -> filter1 (90%) -> [4.5M]
            Processes 10M + 5M + 4.5M = 19.5M operations
            (5.5M fewer operations!)

questions.question: A developer replaces a traditional loop with Streams but the code becomes harder to read. In what scenarios should Streams be avoided?
questions.answer:
Avoid streams when: (1) Complex imperative logic (multiple variables, state changes) - harder to express in streams, (2) Debugging - single breakpoint in loop vs tracing through pipeline, (3) Performance-critical hot paths with simple loops - overhead of stream abstraction matters, (4) Early termination with side effects - awkward to express in streams.

Prefer loops for: Modifying collection in-place (streams are immutable), multiple independent iterations on same data, nested complex conditions, mutation-heavy logic. Streams are best for: data transformation pipelines, filtering + mapping, parallel processing, composing reusable operations.
questions.diagrams:
Stream vs Loop Use Cases:
Streams Excel:
[Data] -> filter -> map -> collect -> Result
Simple, declarative, readable

Loops Excel:
[Complex State Machine]
Multiple conditions, mutations, branches
Easier to follow imperative flow

Use Streams: filter, map, reduce, group
Use Loops: Nested conditions, mutations, early breaks

questions.question: Can Streams modify the original collection?
questions.answer:
Streams themselves cannot modify the original collection. However, if stream operations involve side effects (modifying objects referenced by stream), those changes affect the original. Example: stream of objects, map calls setters on those objects - the original collection sees those changes.

Best practice: Avoid side effects in streams. Streams are meant for immutable transformations. If you need to modify originals, use a loop instead.
questions.diagrams:
Stream vs Original Collection:
Original List: [User1, User2, User3]
                 |
            Stream of references
                 |
           forEach(u -> u.setActive())
                 |
           User1 object MODIFIED
           User2 object MODIFIED
           User3 object MODIFIED
                 |
Original List now: [User1(modified), User2(modified), User3(modified)]

questions.question: What is the difference between map() and flatMap() conceptually? When would you prefer flatMap?
questions.answer:
map(Function<T, R>): Transforms each element T into R. One input element produces one output element. Returns Stream<R>.

flatMap(Function<T, Stream<R>>): Transforms each element T into a Stream<R>, then flattens all streams into one. One input element can produce zero, one, or many output elements. Useful when transformation produces multiple results per element.

Use flatMap when: (1) Each element expands into multiple elements (list of lists flattened), (2) Transformation returns optional (filter out nulls), (3) Parsing produces multiple objects per input.
questions.diagrams:
map() vs flatMap():

map:
input: [1, 2, 3]
  |
  v
  map(x -> x * 2)
  |
 output: [2, 4, 6]

flatMap:
input: [[a, b], [c, d], [e]]
  |
  v
  flatMap(list -> list.stream())
  |
 output: [a, b, c, d, e]

One-to-many:
input: [1, 2, 3]
  |
  v
  flatMap(x -> [x, x*2, x*3])
  |
 output: [1, 2, 3, 2, 4, 6, 3, 6, 9]

questions.question: Why is forEach() considered a terminal operation while peek() is intermediate?
questions.answer:
Terminal operations return a value or void and close the stream (end of pipeline). forEach(Consumer) performs action on each element and returns void - it's the endpoint. No further operations can follow.

Intermediate operations return Stream and keep pipeline open. peek(Consumer) performs action on each element but returns the same Stream unchanged - it's meant for debugging, not consuming.

Distinction: peek is for observation (side effects for debugging like logging), forEach is for consumption (main processing logic). peek doesn't consume stream; forEach does.
questions.diagrams:
Stream Pipeline Ends:
[Source] -> [filter] -> [map] -> [forEach] TERMINAL
                                     |
                                 EXHAUSTED
                                 No more ops

[Source] -> [filter] -> [peek] -> [map] -> [collect] TERMINAL
                          |
                      Returns same Stream
                      Pipeline continues

questions.question: Why is Stream not a data structure?
questions.answer:
Streams are abstractions representing a sequence of operations, not actual data containers like List or Array. Streams don't store data; they describe transformations on source data. The source (List, array, generator) holds data; Stream is a pipeline over it.

Consequences: (1) No indexing (no get(i)), (2) No modification of stream itself, (3) Lazy evaluation (no data until consumed), (4) No traversal without terminal operation, (5) Can be from infinite sources (infinite generators).
questions.diagrams:
Stream vs Data Structure:

List (Data Structure):
[1, 2, 3] <-- Actual storage
  |
  v
Can access, modify, traverse multiple times

Stream (Pipeline):
[1, 2, 3] <-- Source
  |
[filter] --> [map] --> [collect]
             (operations, not data)
             |
Consume once, then exhausted

questions.question: What happens if we modify the source collection while the stream is being processed?
questions.answer:
Modifying the source collection during stream processing (adding/removing elements) causes ConcurrentModificationException if structural changes occur. Modifying existing elements is safe (changes reflected in stream if they haven't been processed yet).

Reason: Stream iterator maintains internal state; structural changes invalidate this state. Streams expect source to remain stable during processing.

Solution: Don't modify source collection during streaming. If modification is needed, complete stream first, or use a copy of the collection.
questions.diagrams:
Stream State During Processing:
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
     ^
     | Iterator at position 2
     |
If you remove element here:
Iterator state becomes invalid
--> ConcurrentModificationException

Correct Flow:
[Process stream] --> [Complete] --> [Modify source safely]

questions.question: Can a Stream pipeline run without a terminal operation?
questions.answer: No, nothing happens without a terminal operation. Intermediate operations are lazy and only build up a chain. Without a terminal operation, the chain is never executed. This is by design - avoids unnecessary processing of intermediate results that aren't consumed.
questions.diagrams:
Pipeline Execution Without Terminal:
[filter] -> [map] -> (stops here, nothing happens)
           (chain built, no execution)

Pipeline Execution With Terminal:
[filter] -> [map] -> [forEach] TRIGGER
             ^
       (executes entire chain)

questions.question: What is Parallel Stream and how does Java decide how many threads to use in a parallel stream?
questions.answer:
Parallel stream divides work across multiple threads using ForkJoinPool. Calls to parallelStream() or .parallel() enable multi-threaded processing. Java uses ForkJoinPool.commonPool() by default (threads = Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors()).

ForkJoinPool uses work-stealing: threads process assigned work units, and idle threads steal incomplete units from busy threads. This balances load automatically.

Note: Number of threads is fixed (processor count) and shared across entire JVM. Multiple parallel streams from different threads compete for same pool - can cause resource contention.
questions.diagrams:
Parallel Stream Execution:
[Data] [2, 4, 6, 8]
        |
        v
[ForkJoinPool]
   /    |    \
[T1]  [T2]  [T3]  [T4]
 |     |     |     |
Work stealing when one thread finishes
|
[Combine results]
|
[Final output]

questions.question: What kind of operations are not suitable for Parallel Streams?
questions.answer:
Unsuitable for parallel: (1) Operations with side effects (modifying shared state, I/O) - race conditions, (2) Operations dependent on order - parallel loses sequential guarantee, (3) Small datasets - overhead of thread management exceeds benefit, (4) Stateful operations like distinct(), sorted() - must see all elements, limiting parallelization, (5) Operations on Sequential-only sources like Iterator.

Better parallel candidates: Independent transformations (filter, map) on large immutable data with no side effects and order-independence.
questions.diagrams:
Parallel Stream Suitability:
Suitable: Large dataset + Independent operations + No order requirement
[Huge data] -> parallelStream() -> Big speedup

Unsuitable: Small dataset or Dependent ops or Order matters
[Small data] -> parallelStream() -> Slower (overhead)
[10M records with distinct()] -> Must process all, no parallelization

questions.question: Parallel Streams internally use which thread pool? Can it affect other tasks?
questions.answer:
Parallel streams use ForkJoinPool.commonPool() by default, shared across entire JVM. When parallel stream runs, it claims threads from this shared pool. If another parallel stream or ForkJoinTask runs simultaneously, they compete for same threads.

Impact: (1) If commonPool is exhausted, new parallel tasks wait, (2) Long-running parallel stream can starve other tasks, (3) No priority mechanism - FIFO, (4) All threads in pool are generic - no specialization.

Mitigation: Use custom ForkJoinPool for important tasks instead of relying on commonPool.
questions.diagrams:
ForkJoinPool Sharing:
[commonPool: 8 threads]
   /        |        \
[T1][T2][T3][T4][T5][T6][T7][T8]
  |
  +-- parallelStream 1 (uses 4 threads)
  |
  +-- parallelStream 2 (uses 3 threads)
  |
  +-- parallelStream 3 (waiting, no threads available)

Custom pools (isolated):
[customPool1: 4 threads] -- parallelStream 1
[customPool2: 2 threads] -- parallelStream 2

questions.question: If a stream operation depends on previous results, should we use Parallel Stream? Why?
questions.answer:
No, avoid parallel streams when operations depend on previous results. Parallel destroys order and assumes independence. If result of element N depends on processing of element N-1, parallelization is impossible.

Examples of order-dependent: Running sum (each element adds to previous total), filtering based on previous state, stateful transformations. These must be processed sequentially.
questions.diagrams:
Order-Dependent vs Independent:

Order-Dependent (Sequential required):
Element 1 -> [Process] -> Generate value1
Element 2 -> [Process using value1] -> Generate value2
Element 3 -> [Process using value2] -> Generate value3
            ^
       Must be sequential

Order-Independent (Parallel OK):
Element 1 -> [Process] -> value1
Element 2 -> [Process] -> value2
Element 3 -> [Process] -> value3
            ^
       Can process in any order

questions.question: When should we prefer sequential streams over parallel streams?
questions.answer:
Prefer sequential when: (1) Dataset is small (<1000 elements), (2) Operations are cheap (filter, map of simple logic), (3) Order matters (need deterministic output), (4) Operations are stateful (distinct, sorted, reduce with order-dependency), (5) Stream source doesn't parallelize well (LinkedList, Iterator), (6) Running on single-core system, (7) Operations have side effects.

Parallel shines on: Large datasets, expensive operations, order-independent transformations, multi-core systems.
questions.diagrams:
Sequential vs Parallel Decision Tree:

                [Stream operation]
                      |
        [Dataset size?]
        /              \
    Small            Large
      |                |
   SEQ              [Operations?]
                   /          \
            Cheap/Stateful  Expensive/Independent
              |               |
            SEQ             PARALLEL

questions.question: What makes an interface a Functional Interface? Can it have multiple methods?
questions.answer:
Functional Interface: Exactly one abstract method (can have default/static methods). The @FunctionalInterface annotation is optional but recommended for compile-time checking.

Example: Runnable (void run()), Comparator (int compare(T, T)), Function<T, R> (R apply(T)). Multiple default methods are allowed as long as only one abstract method exists.

Multiple abstract methods violate functional interface contract and prevent lambda expression usage.

questions.question: Why does Java allow multiple default methods in a functional interface but only one abstract method?
questions.answer:
Default methods have implementations and don't need to be overridden - they're not abstract. Multiple defaults just provide utility methods, not requiring lambda override. The single abstract method defines the contract - what the lambda must implement.

Default methods enable interface evolution: new versions can add methods without breaking old implementations. Only the single abstract method must be implemented by lambda.
questions.diagrams:
Functional Interface with Defaults:
interface Processor {
  |-- process(T) [ABSTRACT] <-- Lambda implements
  |-- validate() [DEFAULT] <-- Inherited
  |-- helper() [DEFAULT] <-- Inherited
}

Lambda: (T) -> {...} <- Implements only abstract

Inheritance chain:
Lambda IS-A Processor
  -> can call process()
  -> can call validate() via default
  -> can call helper() via default
